steps.title: How the National Grid Works
steps.content:
The **National Grid** transmits electricity from power stations to homes and businesses.

Electricity is transmitted at **very high voltage** (e.g. 400 000 V) and **low current** to reduce **power loss** in cables.

Power loss in cables: P = I²R — higher current means much more energy wasted as heat.

**Step-up transformers** increase voltage before transmission.
**Step-down transformers** reduce voltage to safe levels for homes (230 V).

steps.title: The National Grid
steps.question: Why is electricity transmitted at high voltage and low current in the National Grid?
options: To reduce power loss as heat in the cables
steps.correctFeedback: Correct! P = I²R — power loss is proportional to the square of the current. Transmitting at high voltage (and therefore low current) dramatically reduces energy lost as heat in cables.
steps.misconceptionFeedback: The key equation is P = I²R. Since resistance (R) of cables is fixed, reducing current (I) reduces power loss much more effectively than reducing voltage. High voltage → low current → less energy wasted as heat.
steps.bestSource: AQA GCSE Physics — Magnetism and Electromagnetism
